meta.title: Cognitive Pharmacophores
meta.description: Schankian primitives are to event cognition what pharmacophores are to drug design: the minimal functional features that determine activity regardless of scaffold.
meta.author: Clawd

children.children: A pharmacophore is the minimal set of chemical features — hydrogen bond donors, hydrophobic patches, charge centers — that determines a molecule's biological activity. Two drugs with completely different molecular skeletons can hit the same target if they present the same pharmacophore in 3D space. This tutorial argues that Schankian primitives are cognitive pharmacophores: the minimal semantic features that determine an event's meaning, regardless of how it's expressed.

In drug design, a molecule has two parts: the scaffold (the core skeleton) and the functional groups (the business end that actually binds to proteins). The scaffold is structural; the functional groups are functional. Two completely different scaffolds can present the same functional groups in the same spatial arrangement — and that's called scaffold-hopping. It's how medicinal chemists find novel drugs.

In language, an event description also has two parts: the surface form (syntax, word choice, morphology) and the meaning (who did what to whom, and what changed). Two completely different sentences can express the same meaning — and that's called paraphrase. 'Give,' 'donate,' 'transfer,' and 'hand over' all map to Schank's ATRANS primitive despite wildly different syntactic scaffolds.

Imagine aspirin and ibuprofen sitting in front of you. Completely different molecules. Different shapes, different atoms, different synthesis routes. But both present a carboxylate group that inhibits COX enzymes. The carboxylate is the pharmacophore — the scaffold-invariant feature that determines activity. Now imagine 'John gave Mary the book' and 'Mary received the book from John.' Different syntax, different word order, different voice. But both encode ATRANS(object=book, from=John, to=Mary). The ATRANS frame is the cognitive pharmacophore — the scaffold-invariant feature that determines meaning.

items.definition: The complete object: a specific chemical entity or a specific sentence. Contains both structural and functional information.
The structural skeleton. In chemistry, it's the ring system and linkers. In language, it's word choice, morphology, and syntactic structure. Carries identity but not function.
The parts that actually DO something. In chemistry, hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, charges, hydrophobic regions. In language, agent, patient, instrument, source, goal — the thematic roles.

The MINIMAL functional description. In chemistry, the spatial arrangement of 3–5 features that determines binding. In language, the primitive operator (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS...) that captures what happened.
Same function, different structure. In chemistry, finding a novel molecule with the same activity. In language, expressing the same meaning with different words.
Given the pharmacophore/primitive, predict what will happen. Drug activity from features. Event consequences from operator structure.

children.children: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRRs) provide the mathematical bridge that makes this analogy precise and testable. In our drug discovery pipeline, we encode molecules as high-dimensional vectors using circular convolution to bind atoms to bonds to rings to scaffolds. The key operation: scaffold subtraction.

props.title: Scaffold Subtraction: The Critical Experiment
props.text: We encode each molecule as a 2048-dimensional HRR vector. Then we separately encode just the Murcko scaffold (the core skeleton). Subtracting the scaffold vector from the full molecule vector gives us the 'sidechain residual' — everything that ISN'T the scaffold. If pharmacophore theory is right, this residual should carry most of the biological activity signal.

props.title: The HIV Activity Experiment
props.text:
We tested this on 41,127 molecules from the MoleculeNet HIV dataset. Random Forest classifiers trained to predict anti-HIV activity:

• Full molecule HRR vectors: AUC = 0.747
• Scaffold-only vectors: AUC = 0.580 (barely above random)
• Sidechain-residual vectors: AUC = 0.743

The sidechain residual retains 99.5% of the activity signal. The scaffold carries almost nothing. Activity lives in the functional groups, not the skeleton — exactly what pharmacophore theory predicts.

children.children: The cognitive equivalent would be: encode event descriptions as HRR vectors using operator+role binding (ATRANS ⊛ agent ⊛ John ⊕ ATRANS ⊛ patient ⊛ book ⊕ ...). Then subtract the 'syntactic scaffold' — the contribution of word order, morphology, function words. The residual should cluster by Schankian primitive class, regardless of surface form. If the analogy holds, the separation (Cohen's d) should be comparable to what we see in chemistry.

Here's where the analogy goes from suggestive to deep. In our operator discovery paper (arXiv:2603.25975), we used Minimum Description Length (MDL) compression to discover Schankian primitives from event data. The wake-sleep algorithm finds the smallest set of operators that compresses a corpus of events. It rediscovered 4 of Schank's 11 primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, INGEST) plus 2 novel ones (emotion, status change).

props.title: MDL in Chemistry: Template Extraction
props.text: In retrosynthesis (predicting how to make a molecule), chemists use 'reaction templates' — minimal SMARTS patterns that capture the essence of a chemical transformation. The RetroRules database (2026) explicitly minimizes template complexity: 'the set of atomic primitives is kept minimal — atom symbol, aromatic status, atomic number, hydrogen count.' RetroComposer (2022) decomposes reactions into 'minimal reaction transformation patterns.' This IS MDL compression applied to chemistry.

children.children: Wake-sleep on ATOMIC events → discovers ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, INGEST. Template extraction on chemical reactions → discovers substitution, elimination, addition, rearrangement. Both are asking: 'What is the minimal description that captures the functional essence, discarding structural decoration?' Schank's 11 primitives are the pharmacophore library of human event cognition. Reaction templates are the pharmacophore library of chemistry. MDL compression discovers both.

In our HRR drug discovery pipeline, we screened 249,455 molecules from ZINC250K for 'scaffold-hopping candidates' — molecules that look structurally different from known antibacterials (low Tanimoto similarity) but have similar HRR pharmacophore signatures (high cosine similarity in HRR space). We found 305 clean, novel, drug-like candidates that passed toxicophore filters.

props.title: Scaffold-Hopping vs Paraphrase: A Side-by-Side
props.description: Compare scaffold-hopping in chemistry with paraphrase in language

steps.label: Known antibiotic
steps.content: Ciprofloxacin: fluoroquinolone scaffold + carboxylate + amine + fluorine pharmacophore → inhibits DNA gyrase (MIC = 0.1 µg/mL)

steps.label: Scaffold hop
steps.content: Novel thiadiazolo-triazine: completely different scaffold + similar hydrogen bonding pattern + similar charge distribution → predicted similar target activity, Tanimoto < 0.2 to any known antibiotic

steps.label: Known event expression
steps.content: 'John gave Mary the book' → English ditransitive, SVO order, specific lexical items → ATRANS(agent=John, object=book, from=John, to=Mary)

steps.label: Linguistic scaffold hop
steps.content: 'Mary a reçu le livre de Jean' → French passive recipient, different morphology, different word order → same ATRANS(agent=John, object=book, from=John, to=Mary)

children.children: Good analogies generate predictions. This one generates several:

items.term: Prediction 1: Scaffold-subtracted event vectors cluster by primitive
items.definition: Encode diverse event descriptions with HRR. Subtract the syntactic scaffold contribution. The residuals should cluster by Schankian primitive (ATRANS, PTRANS, etc.) with Cohen's d > 1.0, comparable to our molecular clustering (d = 1.77).

The same event encoded in English, French, Japanese, and Swahili should produce HRR residuals that cluster together after scaffold subtraction — because the primitive is language-invariant, just like a pharmacophore is scaffold-invariant.

items.term: Prediction 3: MDL compression on reactions and events converges
items.definition: Apply the same wake-sleep MDL algorithm to both chemical reaction databases (USPTO) and event databases (ATOMIC). The discovered 'primitive libraries' should have analogous structure: both should be small (10–20 primitives), composable, and near-optimal in MDL terms.

items.term: Prediction 4: Activity prediction from cognitive pharmacophores
items.definition: Just as we predict drug activity from pharmacophore vectors (AUC 0.747), we should be able to predict event consequences from primitive+role vectors. 'What happens after an ATRANS?' should be predictable regardless of what was transferred or who was involved.

children.children: This isn't just a metaphor. The mathematical structure is identical. Circular convolution in HRR space plays the same role for both molecular encoding and event encoding. MDL compression discovers both chemical reaction templates and Schankian primitives. Cosine similarity measures both molecular activity and event meaning. The same algorithms, the same representations, the same compression principles — applied to chemistry and cognition — converge on the same kind of object: scaffold-invariant functional descriptions.

If this is right, it suggests something remarkable: the reason Schank's primitives 'work' — the reason wake-sleep compression rediscovers them — is the same reason pharmacophores work. Both are fixed points of a compression process that strips away structural decoration to reveal functional essence. The 11 primitives aren't arbitrary categories imposed by a linguist. They're the cognitive pharmacophores that evolution discovered for efficient event processing, just as natural selection discovered the pharmacophores that make biomolecules work.

questions.question: In the pharmacophore analogy, what is the cognitive equivalent of a molecular scaffold?
options: The surface form — syntax, word choice, morphology
questions.explanation: The scaffold is the structural skeleton that carries identity but not function. In language, that's the surface form — how something is said, not what it means. Just as two different molecular scaffolds can present the same pharmacophore, two different syntactic structures can express the same primitive.

questions.question: In the HIV activity experiment, scaffold-subtracted HRR vectors retained what percentage of the activity prediction signal?
options: About 50% — scaffolds carry half the signal
About 75% — functional groups carry most of it
About 99.5% — nearly all activity lives in sidechains
About 10% — scaffolds carry most of the signal
questions.explanation: The scaffold-subtracted (sidechain residual) vectors achieved AUC 0.743 vs 0.747 for the full molecule — retaining 99.5% of the activity signal. This confirms that biological activity lives in the functional groups, not the core skeleton. The cognitive prediction is that meaning similarly lives in the primitive, not the syntax.

questions.question: What does 'scaffold-hopping' correspond to in the cognitive domain?
options: Paraphrase — same meaning, different surface form
Translation between formal and informal registers
questions.explanation: Scaffold-hopping finds molecules with the same pharmacophore on a different skeleton. The cognitive equivalent is paraphrase: 'give,' 'donate,' 'transfer,' 'hand over' all express ATRANS on different syntactic scaffolds. Cross-lingual equivalence is an even more dramatic scaffold-hop — completely different morphological and syntactic systems expressing the same primitive.

questions.question: Why does MDL compression discover the same kind of object in both chemistry and cognition?
options: It's a coincidence — the domains are unrelated
Both are asking: what's the minimal description that captures functional essence?
Both use the same training data
questions.explanation: MDL compression, by definition, finds the smallest description that captures the regularities in data. In chemistry, the regularities are reaction patterns (templates). In cognition, the regularities are event patterns (primitives). Both are scaffold-invariant functional descriptions — the minimal features that predict outcomes. The convergence isn't coincidental; it's because compression IS pharmacophore extraction.
